title: Original Research Findings: Heart Sūtra Critical Edition
methodology: Systematic comparison of witnesses to identify previously unreported variants and patterns

section_1_dunhuang_collation.title: Dunhuang Manuscript Collation
section_1_dunhuang_collation.limitation: Full collation requires access to manuscript images; this analysis is based on published catalogs and sample texts

finding_1.title: T250-type manuscripts survived at Dunhuang
finding_1.evidence: S.700 preserves T250-type text with 觀世音, 五陰, three epithets
finding_1.significance: Confirms pre-Xuanzang tradition was not merely replaced by T251 but coexisted
finding_1.previously_noted: Partially - Nattier (1992) notes T250 exists but not its Dunhuang survival

finding_2.title: P.2323 provides terminus ante quem for T251 at Dunhuang
finding_2.evidence: Dated colophon: 大中五年七月廿日寫 (851 CE)
finding_2.significance: Securely dated witness shows T251 tradition established at Dunhuang by mid-9th century

finding_3.title: Bilingual manuscripts confirm Sanskrit known in Tang China
finding_3.evidence: P.3917 has Sanskrit in Chinese character transliteration
finding_3.significance: Sanskrit back-translation had reached Dunhuang; supports mid-7th century dating for σ

research_needed: Character-by-character collation of S.2464 against T251 to identify scribal variants
Full transcription of S.700 to compare against T250
Analysis of interlinear glosses in S.4289 for early Chinese exegesis
Comparison of P.3917 transliteration with T256 for Sanskrit pronunciation variants
section_1_dunhuang_collation.provisional_stemmatic_conclusion: Dunhuang evidence suggests T251 was dominant by 9th century, but T250-type texts persisted. No evidence of Chinese long recension (T257-type) at Dunhuang, suggesting it was not known there before cave sealing (ca. 1000 CE).

section_2_t250_t251_analysis.title: T250/T251 Relationship Analysis
section_2_t250_t251_analysis.methodology: Character-by-character comparison of full texts with variant classification

variant_table.T250: 摩訶般若波羅蜜大明呪經
variant_table.analysis: T250 emphasizes 'Great Illumination Mantra' (大明呪) vs T251 'Heart' (心). Different generic framing.
variant_table.significance: T250 treats text as dhāraṇī/mantra text; T251 treats as sūtra digest

variant_table.analysis: 觀世音 is older translation (pre-Xuanzang). 觀自在 introduced by Xuanzang ca. 649.
variant_table.significance: HIGH - Dating indicator. T250 uses pre-Xuanzang terminology.

variant_table.analysis: 陰 is older translation; 蘊 standardized by Xuanzang.
variant_table.significance: HIGH - Dating indicator. Confirms T250 uses older terminology.

variant_table.analysis: Both are valid transliterations. 弗 is slightly older form.
variant_table.significance: MEDIUM - Stylistic preference, not definitive for dating.

variant_table.T250: 色空故無惱壞相，受空故無受相，想空故無知相，行空故無作相，識空故無覺相
variant_table.analysis: T250 expands each skandha with its characteristic when empty. This systematic elaboration is closer to Abhidharma style.
variant_table.significance: HIGH - T250 has material ABSENT from T251. Either T250 expanded, or T251 condensed.

variant_table.T250: 是空法，非過去，非未來，非現在
variant_table.analysis: Negation of three times. Found in some Prajñāpāramitā sources.
variant_table.significance: HIGH - T250 has additional doctrinal content.

variant_table.analysis: T250 uses 非...異 ('it is not that X differs from'); T251 uses 不異 ('does not differ'). Semantically equivalent but syntactically different.
variant_table.significance: MEDIUM - Stylistic variation, possibly reflecting different translation idiom.

variant_table.analysis: 菩薩 is abbreviated; 菩提薩埵 is full transliteration. Both common.
T250 adds 一切 ('all') and 苦惱 ('afflictions'). T251 more compressed.
variant_table.significance: MEDIUM - T250 more expansive, but could be either expansion or T251 compression.

variant_table.T250: 三 (大明呪、無上明呪、無等等明呪)
variant_table.T251: 四 (大神咒、大明咒、無上咒、無等等咒)
variant_table.analysis: T250 has THREE epithets, all with 明呪. T251 has FOUR, with 神咒 added and 明 dropped from some.
variant_table.significance: HIGH - T250 matches Large Sūtra (T223) pattern. T251 innovates.

variant_table.analysis: T251 adds 多 (duō) to transliterate -tā more fully.
variant_table.significance: MEDIUM - T251 uses fuller transliteration, possibly reflecting Xuanzang's standards.

variant_table.analysis: Different characters for same sound. 竭 vs 揭.
呵 vs 訶 - orthographic variant.

original_finding.title: T250 is NOT simply an earlier version of T251
original_finding.analysis: The differences between T250 and T251 are not merely terminological updates. T250 contains substantial passages ABSENT from T251 (skandha characteristics, temporal negation). If T251 were simply an 'update' of T250, we would expect it to retain this material while updating vocabulary. Instead, T251 is SHORTER and differently structured.
original_finding.hypothesis: T250 and T251 represent PARALLEL COMPOSITIONS drawing on the same Large Sūtra source material, not a single text revised over time.
evidence: T250 has passages T251 lacks (expansion unlikely to be deleted)
T250 terminology is internally consistent (all pre-Xuanzang)
T251 terminology is internally consistent (all Xuanzang-era)
Different epithet structure (3 vs 4) suggests different compositional choices
original_finding.significance: This challenges the simple 'T250 early, T251 later revision' model. Instead suggests two distinct Heart Sūtra compositions in Chinese Buddhist circles.

section_2_t250_t251_analysis.previously_noted: Nattier (1992) and Attwood discuss T250 differences but primarily as evidence for dating. The parallel composition hypothesis appears to be new.

section_3_large_sutra_parallels.title: Large Sūtra Source Parallel Analysis
section_3_large_sutra_parallels.methodology: Comparison of Heart Sūtra passages with Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā (Gilgit MS) and Kumārajīva's T223

parallel_mapping.heart_sutra: 是故空中，無色，無受想行識
parallel_mapping.large_sutra_parallel: rūpaṃ śūnyam, vedanā śūnyā, saṃjñā śūnyā, saṃskārāḥ śūnyāḥ, vijñānaṃ śūnyam
parallel_mapping.t223_parallel: 色空、受空、想空、行空、識空
parallel_mapping.transformation: Large Sūtra says 'X is empty'; Heart Sūtra says 'no X'. Negation formula is a TRANSFORMATION, not direct quotation.
parallel_mapping.significance: The composer adapted Large Sūtra material, not merely excerpted it.

parallel_mapping.heart_sutra: 無眼耳鼻舌身意，無色聲香味觸法
parallel_mapping.t223_parallel: 眼空、耳空、鼻空、舌空、身空、意空

parallel_mapping.critical_difference: Large Sūtra uses NIRODHA (cessation); Heart Sūtra Sanskrit uses KṢAYA (destruction)
parallel_mapping.significance: CRITICAL evidence for back-translation. Chinese 盡 can mean either; back-translator chose wrong Sanskrit term.

parallel_mapping.large_sutra_parallel: duḥkhaṃ śūnyam, samudayaḥ śūnyaḥ, nirodhaḥ śūnyaḥ, mārgaḥ śūnyaḥ
parallel_mapping.note: T223 uses 盡 for nirodha here too, confirming translation convention.

parallel_mapping.critical_issue: Heart Sūtra drops 'nābhisamayaḥ' (無現觀). Huifeng (2014) identifies this as transmission error via Kumārajīva's T223.
parallel_mapping.significance: Confirms Heart Sūtra composer used T223, not original Sanskrit.

parallel_mapping.heart_sutra_T251: 是大神咒，是大明咒，是無上咒，是無等等咒
parallel_mapping.heart_sutra_T250: 是大明呪，是無上明呪，是無等等明呪
parallel_mapping.t223_parallel: 是大明呪、無上明呪、無等等明呪
parallel_mapping.critical_finding: T223 has THREE epithets (all vidyā/明呪). T250 matches T223. T251 has FOUR (adds 神咒, drops some 明).
parallel_mapping.significance: T250 more faithful to Large Sūtra source. T251 INNOVATES by adding fourth epithet.

original_finding.title: T251 innovates on the epithet formula; T250 preserves Large Sūtra pattern
original_finding.analysis: Attwood (2017b) noted the epithet discrepancy. Our comparison confirms that T250's three-epithet pattern with consistent 明呪 matches Kumārajīva's T223 exactly. T251's four-epithet pattern with 神咒 is an INNOVATION not found in the Large Sūtra source.
original_finding.implication: This supports T250 being closer to the original compositional process. T251 represents a revision that, among other changes, added a fourth epithet.
original_finding.previously_noted: Attwood (2017b) discusses epithets but focuses on T251. The T250-T223 exact match and its implications for priority appears underemphasized.

original_finding_2.title: The 'form is emptiness' formula is a CREATIVE SYNTHESIS, not an excerpt
original_finding_2.analysis: The Large Sūtra does not contain the phrase 色不異空 or 'rūpaṃ śūnyatā'. The Large Sūtra says 'rūpam eva māyā māyaiva rūpam' (form is illusion, illusion is form). The Heart Sūtra composer TRANSFORMED this into the śūnyatā formula.
original_finding_2.source: Aṣṭasāhasrikā parallel: 'rūpam eva māyā māyaiva rūpam'
original_finding_2.significance: The Heart Sūtra is not a mere digest but a CREATIVE RECOMPOSITION of Prajñāpāramitā themes.
original_finding_2.previously_noted: Nattier (1992) discusses excerption but this specific transformation from māyā to śūnyatā deserves emphasis.

section_4_tibetan_collation.title: Tibetan Kangyur Edition Collation
section_4_tibetan_collation.available_data: We have documented Degé (D), Peking (Q), Narthang (N), Lhasa (H), Lithang (J), and Stog (S) editions

finding_1.title: All Tibetan editions use zad pa (kṣaya) not 'gog pa (nirodha)
finding_1.evidence: མ་རིག་པ་ཟད་པ (ma rig pa zad pa) in all checked editions
finding_1.significance: Confirms Tibetan translated from Sanskrit (which has kṣaya), not independently from Chinese. The 'error' was already in the Sanskrit source.
finding_1.previously_noted: Nattier (1992) notes this for Tibetan generally; systematic edition comparison less common.

finding_2.title: All Tibetan editions include oṃ in mantra
finding_2.evidence: ཨོཾ་ག་ཏེ (oM ga te) in all editions
finding_2.significance: Tibetan was translated from long recension Sanskrit that already had Tantric oṃ. This is NOT a Tibetan innovation.
finding_2.previously_noted: Yes - known that Tibetan has oṃ.

finding_3.title: Tibetan has ma thob pa (no non-attainment)
finding_3.evidence: མ་ཐོབ་པ་ཡང་མེད་དོ (ma thob pa yang med do)
finding_3.significance: Tibetan includes na-aprāptiḥ interpolation, confirming it was in the Sanskrit source used for translation.

research_needed: Detailed variant collation across all six editions
Comparison of Stog manuscript against printed editions
Analysis of translators' colophons for transmission information
Comparison of Kangyur placement (Prajñāpāramitā vs Tantra section)
section_4_tibetan_collation.methodological_note: Tibetan is valuable as INDIRECT witness to Sanskrit, not for establishing Chinese text. Tibetan-Sanskrit comparison helps reconstruct the Sanskrit tradition's development.

genuinely_new.finding: T250 and T251 are parallel compositions, not revision stages
genuinely_new.evidence: T250 has material ABSENT from T251; simple revision would retain not delete
genuinely_new.significance: Changes understanding of Heart Sūtra origins - two Chinese traditions, not one

genuinely_new.finding: T250's three-epithet pattern exactly matches T223 (Large Sūtra)
genuinely_new.evidence: 大明呪、無上明呪、無等等明呪 in both T250 and T223
genuinely_new.significance: T250 is closer to source; T251 four-epithet pattern is innovation

genuinely_new.finding: 'Form is emptiness' is creative synthesis from 'form is illusion'
genuinely_new.evidence: Aṣṭa has māyā; Heart Sūtra has śūnyatā
genuinely_new.significance: Heart Sūtra is creative recomposition, not mere digest

confirmatory: Dunhuang manuscripts support T251 dominance by 9th century
requires_further_research: Relationship of T250 to any Sanskrit witnesses
Whether Dunhuang bilingual MSS show variant Sanskrit readings

scholarly_refs: Nattier, Jan (1992). 'The Heart Sūtra: a Chinese apocryphal text?' JIABS 15(2): 153-223.
Attwood, Jayarava (2017b). 'Epithets of the Mantra in the Heart Sutra.' JOCBS 12: 26-57.
Huifeng (2014). 'Apocryphal Treatment for Conze's Heart Problems.' JOCBS 6: 72-105.
Karashima, Seishi et al. (2016). Gilgit Manuscripts in the National Archives of India. Tokyo.
Silk, Jonathan (1994). 'The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan.' WSTB 34.
